extra.reasoning:
The property "globally(opted_out implies not sent)" is an inductive invariant maintained by every transition.

Key observations:
1. The ONLY transition that sets sent := true is t2 (on send_attempt if NOT opted_out). Its guard requires opted_out = false, and it does not modify opted_out, so after t2 fires opted_out is still false. Thus whenever sent becomes true, opted_out is false (invariant holds vacuously).
2. The ONLY transition that sets opted_out := true is t1 (on optout if true). t1 simultaneously sets sent := false. So the moment opted_out flips to true, sent is forced false (invariant holds).
3. Every other transition (t3-t26) sets sent := false and never changes opted_out; t27 (send_attempt while opted_out) sets sent := false. None of these can create a state with opted_out true and sent true.

Inductive check of the invariant P = (opted_out => not sent):
- Initial state: opted_out=false, sent=false => P holds.
- t1: opted_out:=true, sent:=false => P holds.
- t2: guard not opted_out, opted_out stays false, sent:=true => opted_out false => P holds vacuously.
- t27: opted_out unchanged, sent:=false => P holds.
- t3-t26: sent:=false, opted_out unchanged => P holds.

Since no reachable transition can produce (opted_out=true, sent=true), the invariant holds on all reachable states, and therefore on every execution (well within the 11-event bound). The property holds; no counterexample exists.
